title: Noisy Data? Python Has a Filter for That

yaml.title: Noisy Data? Python Has a Filter for That
yaml.subtitle: A practical intro to Kalman filters

markdown.markdown:
```{python}
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)

t    = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
true = 40 + 5 * np.sin(t) + np.cumsum(np.random.randn(len(t)) * 0.2)
meas = true + np.random.normal(0, 5, size=t.shape)
```



This is pretty close to what I do every day.

How do you estimate something you can't directly observe — when your data is noisy?


## The Problem

- We observe the world through sensors
- Sensors are imperfect
- Data is messy

. . .

**In our lab:** we measure NO2 across a city with low-cost sensors.

They're cheap enough to deploy everywhere — but noisy.


## Example: Noisy NO2 Signal

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.plot(t, meas, '.', color="tomato", label="Low-cost sensor", alpha=0.7)
ax.plot(t, true, '-', color="white",  label="True NO2",        linewidth=2)
ax.set_ylabel("NO2 (μg/m³)")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```


## What Do We Want?

- Estimate the **hidden state**
- Reduce noise
- Do it in a principled way


## Intuition

We have two sources of information:

- Prediction (model)
- Measurement (noisy)

. . .

👉 The key idea: combine them based on **uncertainty**


## The Core Idea

::: {.r-fit-text}
Predict → Measure → Correct
:::


## State Model

$$x_k = A \, x_{k-1} + w_k$$

We predict the next state with some uncertainty.


## Measurement Model

$$z_k = H \, x_k + v_k$$

We observe a noisy version of the state.


## Update Step

$$x_k = x_k^- + K_k \underbrace{(z_k - H x_k^-)}_{\text{innovation}}$$

Prediction + correction

. . .

👉 Correction depends on how much we trust the measurement


## Why Not Just Use a Library?

We could…

But we needed:

- More control over the model
- Access to internals
- To extend the method

. . .

👉 So we built a small version ourselves


## Vanilla Python Implementation

```{python}
#| echo: true
x = 40.0   # initial guess (NO2 in μg/m³)
P = 10.0   # initial uncertainty

A = 1      # NO2 changes slowly
H = 1      # we measure it directly
Q = 0.3    # process noise
R = 25.0   # sensor noise variance (std ≈ 5 μg/m³)

estimates = []

for z in meas:
    # Predict
    x = A * x
    P = A * P * A + Q

    # Update
    K = P / (P + R)       # Kalman gain
    x = x + K * (z - x)
    P = (1 - K) * P

    estimates.append(x)

estimates = np.array(estimates)
```


## Result: Filtering

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.plot(t, meas,      '.', color="tomato",    label="Sensor",        alpha=0.6)
ax.plot(t, true,      '-', color="white",     label="True NO2",      linewidth=2)
ax.plot(t, estimates, '-', color="limegreen", label="Kalman Filter", linewidth=2.5)
ax.set_ylabel("NO2 (μg/m³)")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```


## This is great… but

Kalman filtering only uses:

👉 **past + current** data


## What If We Have All the Data?

Offline setting — sensor logs processed overnight:

- We already have the full time series
- Can we do better?

. . .

👉 Yes — **smoothing**


## Key Idea: Smoothing

- Forward pass → Kalman filter
- Backward pass → refine estimates

. . .

👉 Use future information to improve past estimates


## RTS Smoother

$$x_k^s = x_k + C_k \left( x_{k+1}^s - x_{k+1}^- \right)$$

👉 We adjust estimates using future information


## RTS Implementation

```{python}
#| echo: true
# Forward pass
xs, Ps, x_preds, P_preds = [], [], [], []
x, P = 40.0, 10.0
for z in meas:
    x_pred = A * x;  P_pred = A * P * A + Q
    K = P_pred / (P_pred + R)
    x = x_pred + K * (z - x_pred);  P = (1 - K) * P_pred
    xs.append(x);  Ps.append(P)
    x_preds.append(x_pred);  P_preds.append(P_pred)

xs, Ps = np.array(xs), np.array(Ps)
x_preds, P_preds = np.array(x_preds), np.array(P_preds)

# Backward pass (RTS)
smoothed = xs.copy()
for k in range(len(xs) - 2, -1, -1):
    C = Ps[k] * A / P_preds[k+1]
    smoothed[k] = xs[k] + C * (smoothed[k+1] - x_preds[k+1])
```


## Result: Smoothing

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4))
ax.plot(t, meas,      '.', color="tomato",    label="Sensor",         alpha=0.5)
ax.plot(t, true,      '-', color="white",     label="True NO2",       linewidth=2)
ax.plot(t, estimates, '-', color="limegreen", label="Filtered",       linewidth=2,   alpha=0.7)
ax.plot(t, smoothed,  '-', color="gold",      label="Smoothed (RTS)", linewidth=2.5)
ax.set_ylabel("NO2 (μg/m³)")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```


## Filtering vs Smoothing

| | Filter | RTS Smoother |
|--|--------|-------------|
| **Data used** | past + present | full series |
| **Use case** | real-time | offline reanalysis |
| **Accuracy** | good | better |

. . .

👉 Same model — just more information


## Libraries

- `pykalman`
- `filterpy`
- `statsmodels`

👉 These implement filtering and smoothing

. . .

But: building it once makes it much clearer


## Fun Fact 🚀

The **Apollo Guidance Computer** used a Kalman filter.

👉 You can read the original code on GitHub.

- ~2 MHz processor
- ~4 KB RAM
- Still doing state estimation

. . .

> If it worked there, it'll work for our NO2 sensors.


## Why Build It Yourself?

- Understand the mechanics
- Debug and trust results
- Extend when needed
- Not everything is exposed in libraries


## Real Data — UCI Air Quality, Italy {.smaller}

Low-cost metal oxide sensor + reference analyser, hourly NO2 readings.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
from scipy import stats

df = pd.read_csv("AirQualityUCI.csv", sep=";", decimal=",",
                 usecols=["NO2(GT)", "PT08.S4(NO2)"])
df.columns = ["no2_ref", "no2_sensor"]
df.replace(-200, np.nan, inplace=True)
df.dropna(inplace=True)
df = df.iloc[:500].reset_index(drop=True)

# Calibrate sensor to reference units
slope, intercept, *_ = stats.linregress(df["no2_sensor"], df["no2_ref"])
sensor_cal = slope * df["no2_sensor"] + intercept
```


## Fusing Real Data

```{python}
x, P, A = float(df["no2_ref"].iloc[0]), 20.0, 1
R_ref, R_sensor, Q = 4.0, 50.0, 0.5
xs, Ps, x_preds, P_preds = [], [], [], []

for z_ref, z_lc in zip(df["no2_ref"], sensor_cal):
    x_pred = x;  P_pred = P + Q
    K = P_pred / (P_pred + R_ref)
    x = x_pred + K * (z_ref - x_pred);  P = (1 - K) * P_pred
    K = P / (P + R_sensor)
    x = x + K * (z_lc - x);  P = (1 - K) * P
    xs.append(x);  Ps.append(P);  x_preds.append(x_pred);  P_preds.append(P_pred)

xs, Ps, x_preds, P_preds = map(np.array, [xs, Ps, x_preds, P_preds])
smoothed_real = xs.copy()
for k in range(len(xs) - 2, -1, -1):
    C = Ps[k] / P_preds[k+1]
    smoothed_real[k] = xs[k] + C * (smoothed_real[k+1] - x_preds[k+1])
```


## Result: Real Fusion

```{python}
t = np.arange(len(df))
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(11, 7))

ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(t, sensor_cal,       color="tomato",    alpha=0.4, linewidth=1,   label="Low-cost (calibrated)")
ax.plot(t, df["no2_ref"],    color="steelblue", linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.8, label="Reference analyser")
ax.plot(t, xs,               color="limegreen", linewidth=2.5,             label="Kalman filtered")
ax.plot(t, smoothed_real,    color="gold",      linewidth=2, linestyle="--", label="RTS smoothed")
ax.fill_between(t, xs - 2*np.sqrt(Ps), xs + 2*np.sqrt(Ps),
                alpha=0.15, color="limegreen", label="±2σ")
ax.set_ylabel("NO2 (μg/m³)")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
ax.set_title("UCI Air Quality — Italy")

ax = axes[1]
err_lc = sensor_cal.values - df["no2_ref"].values
err_kf = xs - df["no2_ref"].values
ax.axhline(0, color="white", linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--")
ax.plot(t, err_lc, color="tomato",    alpha=0.5, linewidth=1,
        label=f"Low-cost  RMSE = {np.sqrt(np.mean(err_lc**2)):.1f} μg/m³")
ax.plot(t, err_kf, color="limegreen", linewidth=1.5,
        label=f"Kalman    RMSE = {np.sqrt(np.mean(err_kf**2)):.1f} μg/m³")
ax.set_xlabel("Hour")
ax.set_ylabel("Error (μg/m³)")
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```


## Back to Our Lab

We run the same approach with our own sensors:

- Multiple **low-cost NO2 sensors** — high density, noisy
- A few **regulatory analysers** — sparse, very accurate

. . .

The filter fuses them: cheap sensors benefit from reference accuracy without being overwritten.


## Takeaways

- Kalman filter = predict + correct
- RTS smoother = refine using future data
- Simple ideas → powerful results

. . .

Build it once from scratch — you'll trust it much more


## Thanks 🙌

Questions?

. . .

Code from this talk: `kalman_talk.qmd`

## Real Data — UCI Air Quality, Italy {.smaller}

Low-cost metal oxide sensor + reference analyser, hourly NO2 readings.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
from scipy import stats

df = pd.read_csv("AirQualityUCI.csv", sep=";", decimal=",",
                 usecols=["NO2(GT)", "PT08.S4(NO2)"])
df.columns = ["no2_ref", "no2_sensor"]
df.replace(-200, np.nan, inplace=True)
df.dropna(inplace=True)
df = df.iloc[:500].reset_index(drop=True)

# Calibrate sensor to reference units
slope, intercept, *_ = stats.linregress(df["no2_sensor"], df["no2_ref"])
sensor_cal = slope * df["no2_sensor"] + intercept
```


## Fusing Real Data

```{python}
x, P, A = float(df["no2_ref"].iloc[0]), 20.0, 1
R_ref, R_sensor, Q = 4.0, 50.0, 0.5
xs, Ps, x_preds, P_preds = [], [], [], []

for z_ref, z_lc in zip(df["no2_ref"], sensor_cal):
    x_pred = x;  P_pred = P + Q
    K = P_pred / (P_pred + R_ref)
    x = x_pred + K * (z_ref - x_pred);  P = (1 - K) * P_pred
    K = P / (P + R_sensor)
    x = x + K * (z_lc - x);  P = (1 - K) * P
    xs.append(x);  Ps.append(P);  x_preds.append(x_pred);  P_preds.append(P_pred)

xs, Ps, x_preds, P_preds = map(np.array, [xs, Ps, x_preds, P_preds])
smoothed_real = xs.copy()
for k in range(len(xs) - 2, -1, -1):
    C = Ps[k] / P_preds[k+1]
    smoothed_real[k] = xs[k] + C * (smoothed_real[k+1] - x_preds[k+1])
```


## Result: Real Fusion

```{python}
t = np.arange(len(df))
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(11, 7))

ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(t, sensor_cal,       color="tomato",    alpha=0.4, linewidth=1,   label="Low-cost (calibrated)")
ax.plot(t, df["no2_ref"],    color="steelblue", linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.8, label="Reference analyser")
ax.plot(t, xs,               color="limegreen", linewidth=2.5,             label="Kalman filtered")
ax.plot(t, smoothed_real,    color="gold",      linewidth=2, linestyle="--", label="RTS smoothed")
ax.fill_between(t, xs - 2*np.sqrt(Ps), xs + 2*np.sqrt(Ps),
                alpha=0.15, color="limegreen", label="±2σ")
ax.set_ylabel("NO2 (μg/m³)")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
ax.set_title("UCI Air Quality — Italy")

ax = axes[1]
err_lc = sensor_cal.values - df["no2_ref"].values
err_kf = xs - df["no2_ref"].values
ax.axhline(0, color="white", linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--")
ax.plot(t, err_lc, color="tomato",    alpha=0.5, linewidth=1,
        label=f"Low-cost  RMSE = {np.sqrt(np.mean(err_lc**2)):.1f} μg/m³")
ax.plot(t, err_kf, color="limegreen", linewidth=1.5,
        label=f"Kalman    RMSE = {np.sqrt(np.mean(err_kf**2)):.1f} μg/m³")
ax.set_xlabel("Hour")
ax.set_ylabel("Error (μg/m³)")
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

We run the same approach with our own sensors:

- Multiple **low-cost NO2 sensors** — high density, noisy
- A few **regulatory analysers** — sparse, very accurate

. . .

The filter fuses them: cheap sensors benefit from reference accuracy without being overwritten.

- Kalman filter = predict + correct
- RTS smoother = refine using future data
- Simple ideas → powerful results

. . .

Build it once from scratch — you'll trust it much more
